does: This paper proposes Defense2Attack, a jailbreak method that exploits weak defense mechanisms in Vision-Language Models to generate more effective adversarial attacks. The core contribution is demonstrating that incorporating defensive patterns into attack design paradoxically strengthens jailbreaks. The method comprises three components: a visual optimizer that embeds adversarial perturbations with positive semantics, a textual optimizer using defense-styled prompts, and a red-team suffix generator enhanced through reinforcement fine-tuning. The authors evaluate their approach on four VLMs across four safety benchmarks, showing improved effectiveness and efficiency compared to existing jailbreak methods.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of jailbreak attacks against large language and vision-language models, extending prior research that identified vulnerabilities in safety alignment mechanisms. It relates to research on adversarial examples in vision systems and prompt injection attacks in language models, synthesizing these domains for multimodal systems. The paper connects to defense mechanisms and safety guardrails developed for VLMs, strategically reversing them for attack purposes. It also engages with reinforcement learning-based optimization methods for generating adversarial content, following recent trends in using RL for attack refinement rather than just reward model optimization.
enables: This research points toward deeper understanding of the adversarial landscape in VLMs and the interplay between defense and attack mechanisms. It could motivate the development of more robust safety approaches that don't inadvertently provide structure for attackers to exploit. The Defense2Attack framework may enable investigation of defense mechanisms that are inherently difficult to reverse-engineer or weaponize. Future work could explore whether certain types of defenses are more fundamentally resistant to this attack-through-defense strategy, inform the design of defense mechanisms with adversarial robustness in mind, and extend this approach to other multimodal or language-only systems to identify universal principles of mutual vulnerability between defenses and attacks.
